request.question: What genre does the album "Levitate" come from?

title: Blackheart (album)
Levitate (The Fall album)
Paper Diamond
Kreep (song)
The Fall (band)
American Tragedy Redux
Untitled 07 – 2014 - 2016
Levitate (Hollywood Undead song)
The Marshall Suite
Levitate (Bruce Hornsby album)
sentences: Blackheart is the third studio album by American singer Dawn Richard, which was released on January 15, 2015, by Our Dawn Entertainment.
The album was originally scheduled for an October 2013 release, but pushed back in favor for the production of the third Danity Kane album "DK3".
None of the previously released singles "Judith", "Meteors", "Levitate" and "Valkyrie" can be found on the final track listing.
Levitate is an album (the 19th) by The Fall, released in 1997 on Artful Records.

"Levitate" became the last album to feature two long-time Fall members, drummer Karl Burns and bass player Steve Hanley (whose playing was once described by Smith as the defining element of the group's music).
Alexander Botwin, better known by his stage name Paper Diamond (formerly Alex B), is an American electronic music producer from Colorado.
He signed with Pretty Lights Music in December 2010, and released his first album Levitate on January 25, 2011.
"Kreep" is the second single from rapper Chino XL's debut album "Here to Save You All".
It is produced by the Eric Romero, who uses elements from "In-A-Gadda-Da-Vida" by Iron Butterfly, "Levitate" by Brecker Brothers, and "Repent Walpurgis" by Procol Harum for the drums.
Additionally the song embodies portions from the song "Creep" by rock group Radiohead and "You Are Everything" by The Stylistics.
This is perhaps Chino Xl's best-known song and his most successful, reaching number one the Billboard Bubbling Under R&B Singles Chart.
It remained number one for two weeks and remained on the chart for 23 weeks from July 1996 to January 1997.
"Kreep" also charted on the Billboard Hot Rap Singles Chart, peaking at number 41 and reached its peak on the week of September 21, 1996.
The Fall are an English post-punk band, formed in 1976 in Prestwich, Greater Manchester.
They have undergone many line up changes, with vocalist Mark E. Smith as the founder and only constant member.

The Fall's long term musicians have included drummers Paul Hanley and Karl Burns; guitarists Marc Riley, Craig Scanlon and Brix Smith; and bassist Steve Hanley, whose melodic playing is widely credited with shaping the band's sound from the early 1980s to the mid-1990s.
American Tragedy Redux is the second remix album by American rap rock band Hollywood Undead, taking songs exclusively off the band's 2011 studio album, "American Tragedy", and remixing them.
The first remix album was the Black Dahlia Remixes album, featuring three remixes of Hollywood Undead's single, "Black Dahlia".
The album was released on November 21, 2011 by A&M/Octone Records.
The original tracks on the album, which were on "American Tragedy", were recorded following the induction of Daniel Murillo into the band in early 2010 and lasted until December.
The tracks were then remixed by various DJ's and musicians during the band's "World War III Tour" with Asking Alexandria later in 2011.
The album's first single, "Levitate (Digital Dog club mix)", was released on October 18, 2011, with a music video being released on October 24.

"Untitled 07 | 2014 - 2016" (stylized untitled 07 | 2014 - 2016), single version titled "untitled 07 | levitate", is a song by American hip hop recording artist Kendrick Lamar, taken from his 2016 compilation album, "untitled unmastered.
On March 23, 2016, "untitled 07 | levitate" was released separately on the iTunes Store as the compilation's lead single.
"Levitate (Digital Dog club mix)" is a song by American rock group Hollywood Undead and was released as the lead single from their first remix album, "American Tragedy Redux".
It is the first song on the official track list and was added to rock radio formats on October 18, 2011.
The song "Levitate" was originally featured on Hollywood Undead's latest studio album American Tragedy (album) but was not featured as a single.
The remix of the song (the first version to actually be released as a single) was heavily promoted over the band's Facebook page, where its official video debuted.
A rock version of "Levitate" was also released onto iTunes as a single on
The Marshall Suite is a 1999 album by The Fall (their 20th).
The album builds on the techno-influenced beats of its predecessor "Levitate" (1997), while also returning to a more rockabilly-influenced sound reminiscent of earlier Fall line-ups.
The end result has songs like the catchy "Touch Sensitive", and the strange, complex, thumping jungle beats of "Crying Marshall".
The album was long out of print, but a new 3-disc edition was released in the summer of 2011.
Levitate is the tenth studio album by Bruce Hornsby.
It was Hornsby's third studio album with his touring band, Bruce Hornsby and the Noisemakers, and was his first release with Verve Records.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What genre does the album "Levitate" come from?
hop1_docs: Youth Gone Wild: Heavy Metal Hits of the '80s | Youth Gone Wild: Heavy Metal Hits of the '80s is a series of compilation albums of heavy metal songs of the 1980s, the heyday of the genre.  The title comes from the song by Skid Row, which ironically does not appear in the series.

Sister Hazel (album) | Sister Hazel is the self-titled and debut studio album by Sister Hazel.  It was originally released in 1994 and re-released in 2005.  It is also referred to as "White."  The album varies widely in genre, with elements of traditional rock, southern rock, country, folk, blues, reggae, funk and cajun all represented.  While no singles were released from this album, it does contain an early acoustic version of what would become their first hit, All For You.  The album did not chart in the U.S. or U.K.

Dön Ne Olur | Dön Ne Olur (English: "Come Back, What Does It Matter" ) is the sixth studio album of Ebru Gündeş, the Turkish pop-folk, actress, and television personality.  The album was released in 2000.

Stronger (What Doesn't Kill You) | "Stronger (What Doesn't Kill You)" is a song by American recording artist Kelly Clarkson and the titular song from her fifth studio album, "Stronger" (2011).  Originally titled as "What Doesn't Kill You (Stronger)", the song served as the album's second single on January 17, 2012 through RCA Records.  Written by Jörgen Elofsson, Ali Tamposi, and David Gamson, with additional writing and production by Greg Kurstin, "Stronger" is an uptempo song which draws from the genres of dance-pop and electropop.  Its lyrical content mainly explores themes of empowerment and recovery following a heartbreak, with the chorus inspired by a Friedrich Nietzsche quotation: "That which does not kill us makes us stronger."

Here Come the Drums | Here Come the Drums is the second studio album by Australian band Rogue Traders, released in Australia on 23 October 2005 by Columbia Records.  The album features the Rogue Traders with the then-new vocalist Natalie Bassingthwaighte and songs primarily in the genre of dance-pop, written by Jamie Appleby, Steven Davis and Melinda Appleby.  "Here Come the Drums" debuted in the top ten on the Australian ARIA Albums Chart, and sales were considerably higher than those of the Rogue Traders's debut album, "We Know What You're Up To" (2003).

What Else Does the Time Mean? | What Else Does The Time Mean?  is the 46th album from avant-folk/blues singer/songwriter Jandek, released by Corwood Industries (#0784).  It is his third release in 2006, following January's' "Khartoum Variations" and February's live double-album "Newcastle Sunday".

Nest (band) | Nest is a neofolk / ambient band from Riihimäki, Finland, formed in 1999.  The band's inspiration comes from the Finnish folklore, as well as from the works of J.R.R. Tolkien and the Grimm Brothers.  Kantele, a traditional Finnish musical instrument, is very prominent in the band's music.  They contributed a cover of the song "The Gallant Crow" to the Skepticism tribute album "Entering the Levitation".
hop1_titles: Youth Gone Wild: Heavy Metal Hits of the '80s
What Else Does the Time Mean?

- What Else Does the Time Mean? — [6]: "46th album from avant-folk/blues singer/songwriter Jandek"
- Nest (band) — [7]: "neofolk / ambient band"
- Entering the Levitation — [7]: "Entering the Levitation"

Ambiguities:
- "Levitate" — not mentioned in any passage; only "Entering the Levitation" appears ([7]).
- Sister Hazel (album) — passage says it "varies widely in genre," listing multiple genres (conflicting genre elements) [2].

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state the album titled "Levitate" or its genre.
- Missing: (1) identification of which "Levitate" album (artist/year) and (2) the genre value for that specific "Levitate" album.

Retrieval direction:
- Open "Levitate (disambiguation)" or search that page.
- Fetch "Levitate" album page(s): run query '"Levitate" album Wikipedia'
- Run targeted query: '"Levitate" album "genre"' or '"Levitate" album site:wikipedia.org'
- If known artist suspected, try '"Levitate" "The Fall" album Wikipedia' (or adjust artist name as needed).
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Levitate (The Fall album) | Levitate is an album (the 19th) by The Fall, released in 1997 on Artful Records.  "Levitate" became the last album to feature two long-time Fall members, drummer Karl Burns and bass player Steve Hanley (whose playing was once described by Smith as the defining element of the group's music).
Levitation (album) | Levitation is the tenth studio album by English rock group Hawkwind, released in 1980.  It peaked at No. 21 on the UK Albums Chart.

Levitate (Bruce Hornsby album) | Levitate is the tenth studio album by Bruce Hornsby.  It was Hornsby's third studio album with his touring band, Bruce Hornsby and the Noisemakers, and was his first release with Verve Records.

Levitate (Hollywood Undead song) | "Levitate (Digital Dog club mix)" is a song by American rock group Hollywood Undead and was released as the lead single from their first remix album, "American Tragedy Redux".  It is the first song on the official track list and was added to rock radio formats on October 18, 2011.  The song "Levitate" was originally featured on Hollywood Undead's latest studio album American Tragedy (album) but was not featured as a single.  The remix of the song (the first version to actually be released as a single) was heavily promoted over the band's Facebook page, where its official video debuted.  A rock version of "Levitate" was also released onto iTunes as a single on

The Reverberation Appreciation Society | The Reverberation Appreciation Society is the creator, curator, and producer of annual psychedelic music festivals Levitation Austin, Levitation France, Levitation Chicago, and Levitation Vancouver.  The Reverberation Appreciation Society’s record label started in 2010, with the release of Christian Bland and the Revelators – The Lost Album.  Later releases include albums by Joel Gion of The Brian Jonestown Massacre, Ringo Deathstarr, Wall of Death, Indian Jewelry, Golden Animals, Night Beats, Holy Wave, and The UFO Club (a collaboration between The Black Angels’ Christian Bland and Night Beats’ Lee Blackwell), as well as joint releases with Burger Records.

Levite | A Levite (or Levi) is a Jewish male whose descent is traced by tradition to Levi.  As a surname, Levite status may be indicated by the term "HaLevi", which consists of the Hebrew prefix "ה" "Ha-" ("the") plus "Levi" (Levite).  The daughter of a Levite is a ""Bat Levi"" ("Bat" being Hebrew for "daughter").
King levitation | The King Rising levitation is a levitation illusion developed by and named after Corey King.  Some consider it to be a variation of the Balducci levitation.
